title: Rassolnik
subtitle: Russian pickle and barley soup
excerpt: A clear, pleasantly sour soup of chicken stock, pearl barley, potato, and pickled gherkins. Rassolnik is the Russian soup that makes gherkins the point — sharp, simple, and impossible to tire of.
sourceNotes: Rassolnik takes its name from rassol — the brine from pickled cucumbers, which is added directly to the soup. It is one of the oldest Russian soups, first recorded in the fifteenth century under various names, and was historically made with kidney or offal braised in pickle brine. The modern domestic version uses chicken stock, pearl barley, potato, and pickled cucumbers, with pickle brine added at the end for sourness. Soured cream is served at the table.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze without the gherkin brine and soured cream. Add brine when reheating and cream at the table.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles well. The barley continues to absorb liquid on standing — thin with stock when reheating.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make up to 2 days ahead. Add a splash of water or stock when reheating as the barley swells in the fridge.

content.text: Rassolnik is built around two things: pearl barley for body and gherkins for sourness. The barley swells as it cooks, releasing starch into the broth and thickening it gradually. The gherkins go in towards the end of cooking (added too early they become mushy and lose their texture), and the gherkin brine follows last, giving the soup its defining sharpness.

The brine is the key. Add it a tablespoon at a time and taste after each addition: the soup should be noticeably sour but not aggressive. Too much brine makes the soup unpleasantly sharp; too little and the sourness that defines the dish is absent.

items.prepNote: peeled and diced into 2 cm cubes

content.text: Melt the {{unsalted-butter}} in a large saucepan over medium heat. Cook the {{onion}} and {{carrot}} for 7 minutes until soft. Add the {{tomato-puree}} and cook for 1 minute. Pour in the {{stock-chicken}} and bring to a simmer. Add the {{pearl-barley}}, {{bay-leaves}}, and {{allspice}}. Simmer for 25 minutes.

Add the {{potato}} and simmer for 15 minutes more until the barley and potato are both fully tender. Add the diced {{gherkins}} and simmer for 5 minutes. Remove the bay leaves and allspice. Add the reserved gherkin brine a tablespoon at a time, tasting after each addition, until the soup has a clear, pleasant sourness. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} and {{black-pepper-ground}}. Scatter with {{dill}} and serve with {{soured-cream}} and bread.

items.cause: Old barley, or not enough time
items.fix: Continue simmering and check every 10 minutes. Older pearl barley can take 40 to 45 minutes to fully soften. The soup will naturally thicken further during this time

items.symptom: Soup too sour after adding the brine
items.cause: Added too much brine at once
items.fix: Add a little water and a pinch of sugar to bring the balance back. The brine should be added gradually, tablespoon by tablespoon, with tasting between each addition

items.symptom: Gherkins too soft in the finished soup
items.fix: Add the gherkins only in the last 5 minutes of cooking. They should have texture in the bowl, not dissolve into the broth

content.text: Rassolnik occupies a specific role in Russian soup-eating: it is the sour soup, the one that makes the gherkin and its brine the central flavour rather than a supporting one. It has been eaten in Russia since the fifteenth century, first as a rich meat and offal preparation, then, as it became more widely cooked at home, as a cleaner, more accessible weekday soup. It appears on every Russian restaurant menu alongside borscht, shchi, and solyanka. Of the four, rassolnik is the most quietly distinctive.
